title: Why I stopped explaining my work to strangers at parties
summary: Reflect on a turning point when you decided to stop describing your work in detail to people who had only just asked out of politeness, and what that revealed about attention.
topic: Why I stopped explaining my work in detail to strangers at parties
prompt: Write 3 paragraphs (350–500 words) about a moment you decided to stop explaining your work in detail to strangers at social events, and what that revealed about attention, politeness, and self-presentation. Use the past perfect, present perfect for reflection, a relative clause, and 'however' or 'although'.
hintStarters: It happened at a dinner in Mumbai about two years ago, where I had just spent four minutes explaining my work to a man who, I slowly realised, had asked the question only to fill a silence and was…
If I had been more honest with myself earlier, I would have noticed that almost no one at those events actually wanted the long answer, and what they were politely waiting for was…
Although polite people teach us never to refuse a sincere question, what I have come to recognise is that most 'what do you do' questions are not the question they appear to be, which is why…

hintVocab.meaningVi: mắt đờ ra, không tập trung
đưa ra câu trả lời ngắn
một câu trả lời chung chung
cảm thấy có nghĩa vụ phải
cách tự thể hiện bản thân
một sự rút lui khéo léo
tha cho ai khỏi nghe chi tiết

sampleText:
It happened at a noisy dinner party in Mumbai about two years ago. I had just spent nearly four minutes explaining the actual content of my work to a man I had been introduced to ten minutes earlier, and I noticed, halfway through my third sentence, that his eyes had already begun searching the room for someone he could politely escape to. He had asked the standard question — 'and what do you do?' — and I had taken it seriously, the way I had always taken it. By the time I noticed his attention had left, my answer had been running for so long that stopping suddenly would have felt strange to both of us. I finished the sentence I had started. He nodded, said 'fascinating,' and walked away. I stood there with a small but very clear thought: I had been wrong about this conversation for years.

What I had not understood, until that night, is that 'what do you do' is rarely a request for information. It is a social formula, a polite handshake in spoken form, the equivalent of asking about the weather. People who genuinely want to know how your work actually functions ask a second, more specific question — 'and what does that involve day to day?' or 'how did you end up doing that?' If the second question does not come, the first question was never really asked. Although it took me an embarrassingly long time to learn this, I now understand that giving the long answer to the formal version is not generous; it is a small social misreading. I had been treating a ritual as if it were a sincere inquiry, and the people I was talking to had been politely enduring the result for years.

What I have come to do since then is give a short, accurate answer first and wait to see whether the second question arrives. If it does, I gladly explain more — and the conversation becomes much better because both people have chosen to be in it. If it does not, I move on and ask the other person something specific about their week. However, this small change has had a larger effect than I expected. It has made my parties shorter and warmer. It has made me much more interested in other people's quick answers, because I now know that the brevity is not laziness but politeness. I have also started to notice, more clearly than before, the very small number of people who do ask the second question — and it is from that small number that almost every real friendship I have made in the last two years has started.

sampleGrammarNotes.title: Past perfect continuous for ongoing activity before a turning point
sampleGrammarNotes.bodyVi: 'The people I was talking to had been politely enduring the result for years' và 'I had been wrong about this conversation for years' dùng quá khứ hoàn thành (tiếp diễn và đơn) để mô tả các trạng thái kéo dài đến tận thời điểm chính của câu chuyện. Đây là cấu trúc cốt lõi để dựng cảm giác 'tôi đã không nhận ra suốt bao lâu rồi'.
sampleGrammarNotes.bodyEn: 'The people I was talking to had been politely enduring the result for years' and 'I had been wrong about this conversation for years' use the past perfect (continuous and simple) to describe states that lasted up to the main moment of the story. This is the core structure for the feeling of 'how long I had not been noticing'.

sampleGrammarNotes.title: 'Not X but Y' to redefine something
sampleGrammarNotes.bodyVi: 'the brevity is not laziness but politeness' dùng cấu trúc 'not X but Y' để bác bỏ một cách hiểu sai và đề xuất cách hiểu đúng. Đây là cấu trúc rất mạnh để diễn đạt sự tinh tế trong văn phản tỉnh.
sampleGrammarNotes.bodyEn: 'The brevity is not laziness but politeness' uses the 'not X but Y' structure to reject one interpretation and propose another. This is a very powerful structure for expressing subtlety in reflective writing.

sampleTranslationVi:
Nó xảy ra tại một bữa tiệc tối ồn ào ở Mumbai khoảng hai năm trước. Tôi vừa mới dành gần bốn phút giải thích nội dung thực sự của công việc tôi cho một người đàn ông tôi đã được giới thiệu mười phút trước, và tôi nhận ra, giữa câu thứ ba, rằng mắt anh ấy đã bắt đầu tìm kiếm trong phòng ai đó mà anh có thể lịch sự thoát đi. Anh ấy đã hỏi câu hỏi tiêu chuẩn — 'và bạn làm gì?' — và tôi đã coi nó nghiêm túc, theo cách tôi đã luôn coi nó. Đến lúc tôi nhận ra sự chú ý của anh đã rời đi, câu trả lời của tôi đã kéo dài quá lâu đến mức dừng đột ngột sẽ cảm thấy lạ với cả hai chúng tôi. Tôi kết thúc câu tôi đã bắt đầu. Anh ấy gật đầu, nói 'thú vị,' và bước đi. Tôi đứng đó với một suy nghĩ nhỏ nhưng rất rõ ràng: tôi đã sai về cuộc trò chuyện này trong nhiều năm.

Điều tôi đã không hiểu, cho đến tối hôm đó, là 'bạn làm gì' hiếm khi là một yêu cầu thông tin. Đó là một công thức xã hội, một cái bắt tay lịch sự dưới dạng lời nói, tương đương với việc hỏi về thời tiết. Những người thực sự muốn biết công việc của bạn vận hành như thế nào sẽ hỏi câu hỏi thứ hai, cụ thể hơn — 'và điều đó bao gồm những gì trong ngày?' hay 'làm thế nào bạn lại làm việc đó?' Nếu câu hỏi thứ hai không đến, câu hỏi thứ nhất chưa bao giờ thực sự được hỏi. Mặc dù tôi mất một thời gian dài đáng xấu hổ để học điều này, tôi giờ hiểu rằng đưa ra câu trả lời dài cho phiên bản hình thức không phải là hào phóng; đó là một sự đọc sai xã hội nhỏ. Tôi đã đối xử với một nghi thức như thể nó là một câu hỏi chân thành, và những người tôi đang nói chuyện đã lịch sự chịu đựng kết quả trong nhiều năm.

Điều tôi đã dần làm kể từ đó là đưa ra một câu trả lời ngắn, chính xác trước và chờ xem liệu câu hỏi thứ hai có đến. Nếu nó đến, tôi vui vẻ giải thích thêm — và cuộc trò chuyện trở nên tốt hơn nhiều bởi vì cả hai người đã chọn ở trong đó. Nếu không, tôi đi tiếp và hỏi người kia điều gì đó cụ thể về tuần của họ. Tuy nhiên, sự thay đổi nhỏ này đã có tác động lớn hơn tôi mong đợi. Nó khiến các buổi tiệc của tôi ngắn hơn và ấm áp hơn. Nó khiến tôi quan tâm nhiều hơn đến những câu trả lời nhanh của người khác, bởi vì tôi giờ biết rằng sự ngắn gọn không phải là lười biếng mà là lịch sự. Tôi cũng đã bắt đầu để ý, rõ ràng hơn trước, đến số rất nhỏ những người thực sự hỏi câu hỏi thứ hai — và từ số nhỏ đó mà gần như mọi tình bạn thực sự tôi đã xây dựng trong hai năm qua đã bắt đầu.

mcQuestions.prompt: Which option uses the past perfect continuous correctly?
options: The people I was talking to has been politely enduring the result for years.
The people I was talking to had been politely endure the result for years.
The people I was talking to had been politely enduring the result for years.
The people I was talking to had being politely enduring the result for years.

mcQuestions.prompt: Which sentence uses 'not X but Y' correctly?
options: The brevity is no laziness but politeness.
The brevity is not laziness although politeness.
The brevity is not laziness however politeness.
The brevity is not laziness but politeness.
mcQuestions.explanation: 'Not X but Y' is the fixed structure for redefining something; 'no' instead of 'not' is wrong; 'although' and 'however' would create different constructions.

options: Although it took me an embarrassingly long time to learn this, I now understand the difference.
Although it took me an embarrassingly long time to learn this, but I now understand the difference.
Although, it took me an embarrassingly long time to learn this I now understand the difference.
It took me an embarrassingly long time to learn this, although however I now understand the difference.
mcQuestions.explanation: 'Although + clause, main clause' — no 'but', no comma after 'although', no doubling.

mcQuestions.prompt: Which sentence uses 'would have felt' correctly?
options: Stopping suddenly would feel strange to both of us at that point.
Stopping suddenly would have felt strange to both of us.
Stopping suddenly would have feel strange to both of us.
Stopping suddenly will have felt strange to both of us.
mcQuestions.explanation: 'Would have + past participle' is the past hypothetical structure; 'felt' is the past participle of 'feel'.

options: Wait to see if or not the second question arrives.
Wait to see whether or not does the second question arrive.
Wait to see whether the second question arrives.
Wait to see whether the second question will arrive or not arrives.
mcQuestions.explanation: 'Whether + clause' in normal subject-verb order is correct. 'If or not' is incorrect; 'whether or not' would be acceptable but option C inverts the verb wrongly.
mcQuestions.hint: 'Whether + subject + verb' in normal order; do not invert.

mcQuestions.prompt: Which option correctly uses 'have come to do' for a continuing change?
options: What I came to do since then is give a short, accurate answer first.
What I have came to do since then is give a short, accurate answer first.
What I have come doing since then is give a short, accurate answer first.
What I have come to do since then is give a short, accurate answer first.
mcQuestions.explanation: Present perfect 'have come' + 'to + base verb'; past participle of 'come' is 'come'.

mcQuestions.prompt: Which sentence uses 'the way I had always taken it' correctly?
options: I had taken it seriously, the way I had always taken it.
I had taken it seriously, the way I always have taken it.
I had taken it seriously, the way I had always took it.
I had taken it seriously, the way I has always taken it.
mcQuestions.explanation: Past perfect 'had + past participle' for an established habit before a past moment; 'taken' is the past participle of 'take'.

mcQuestions.prompt: Which option uses 'larger effect than I expected' correctly?
options: This small change has had a larger effect that I expected.
This small change has had a larger effect than I expected.
This small change has had a larger effect than I expect now.
This small change has had a larger effect than I expecting.
mcQuestions.explanation: Comparative 'larger … than'; past simple 'expected' for the prediction at that time.
mcQuestions.hint: 'Larger … than'; past simple for past expectation.

mcQuestions.prompt: Which sentence uses 'it is from … that …' (cleft sentence) correctly?
options: It is from that small number what almost every real friendship I have made has started.
It is from that small number that almost every real friendship I have made has start.
It is from that small number that almost every real friendship I have made has started.
It from that small number that almost every real friendship I have made has started.
mcQuestions.explanation: Cleft structure 'It is from + noun + that + clause' emphasises the source; 'that' is the connector, not 'what'.
mcQuestions.hint: Cleft sentence: 'It is + X + that + clause'.

mcQuestions.prompt: Which option uses 'have made' (present perfect) correctly?
options: Almost every real friendship I made in the last two years has started from that small number.
Almost every real friendship I have making in the last two years has started from that small number.
Almost every real friendship I has made in the last two years has started from that small number.
Almost every real friendship I have made in the last two years has started from that small number.
mcQuestions.explanation: Present perfect 'have made'; 'I' takes 'have', not 'has'; past participle of 'make' is 'made'.
mcQuestions.hint: 'I have made'; past participle of 'make' is 'made'.

mcQuestions.prompt: Which sentence uses 'have begun + -ing' (present perfect) correctly?
options: I have also started to notice the small number of people who do ask the second question.
I have also start to notice the small number of people who do ask the second question.
I has also started to notice the small number of people who do ask the second question.
I have also started noticing to the small number of people who do ask the second question.
mcQuestions.explanation: Present perfect 'have started' + 'to + base verb'. 'Start to + base verb' and 'start + -ing' are both correct, but 'started noticing to' is wrong word order.

criticalThinkingQuestion: If most polite questions are rituals rather than sincere requests, does answering them literally — with full information — actually make us harder to befriend? Where would you draw the line between honest answers and social skill?
